html:
IX

Cope on the Edge of Things

The adventurer in Duneland hardly knows, as he works his way through one of the infrequent тАЬblowouts,тАЭ whether to thank Nature for her aid or to tax her with her cruelty. She offers few other means of reaching the water save for these nicks in the edges of the great cup; yet it is possible enough to view her as a careless and reckless handmaiden busily devastating the cosmical china-closet. The тАЬblowoutтАЭ is a tragedy, and the cause of further tragedy. The north winds, in the impetus gathered through a long, unimpeded flight over three hundred miles of water, ceaselessly try and test the sandy bulwarks for a slightest opening. The flaw once found, the work of devastation and desolation begins; and, once begun, it continues without cessation. Every hurricane cuts a wider and deeper gash, fills the air with clouds of loose sand, and gives sinister addition to the white shifting heaps and fields that steal slowly yet unrelentingly over the green hinterland of forest which lies below the southern slopes. Trees yet to die stand in passive bands at their feet; the stark, black trunks of trees long dead rise here and there in spots where the sand-glacier has done its work of ruin and passed on.

After some moments of scrambling and panting our two travelers gained the divide. Below them sloped a great amphitheatre of sand, falling in irregular gradations; and at the foot of all lay the lake, calmly azure, with its horizon, whether near or far for it was almost impossible to sayтБатАФmystically vague. On either hand rose other hills of sand, set with sparse pines and covered, in patches, with growths of wild grape, the fruit half ripened. Within the amphitheatre, at various levels, rose grimly a few stumps and shreds of cedars long dead and long indifferent to the future ravages of the enemy. The whole scene was, today, plausibly gentle and inert. It was indeed a bridal of earth and sky, with the self-contained approval of the blue deep and no counter-assertion from any demon wind.

тАЬSo far, so good,тАЭ said Randolph, taking off his hat, wiping his forehead, and breathing just a little harder than he liked. тАЬThe rest of our course is plain: down those slopes, and then a couple of miles along the shore. Easy walking, that; a mere promenade on a boulevard.тАЭ

Cope stood on the height, and tossed his bare head like a tireless young colt. The sun fell bright on his mane of yellow hair. He took in a deep breath. тАЬItтАЩs good!тАЭ he declared. тАЬItтАЩs great! And the water looks better yet. Shall we make it in a rush?тАЭ

He began to plunge down the long, broken sand-slope. Each step was worth ten. Randolph followedтБатАФwith judgment. He would not seem young enough to be a competitor, nor yet old enough to be a drag. On the shore he wiped and panted a little moreтБатАФbut not to the point of embarrassment, and still less to the point of mortification. After all, he was keeping up pretty well.

At the bottom Cope, with his shoes full of sand, turned round and looked up the slope down which his companion was coming. He waved his arms. тАЬItтАЩs almost as fine from here!тАЭ he cried.

The beach, once gained, was in sight both ways for miles. Not a human habitation was visible, nor a human being. Two or three gulls flew a little out from shore, and the tracks of a sandpiper led from the wet shingle to the first fringe of sandgrass higher up.

тАЬWhere are the crowds?тАЭ asked Cope, with a sonorous shout.

тАЬMiles behind,тАЭ replied Randolph. тАЬWe havenтАЩt come this long distance to meet them after all. Besides,тАЭ he continued, looking at his watch, тАЬthis is not the time of day for them. At twelve-fifteen people are not strolling or tramping; theyтАЩre thinking of their dinner. We have a full hour or more for making less than two easy miles before we reach ours.тАЭ

тАЬNo need to hurry, then.тАЭ

The beach, at its edge, was firm, and they strolled on for half a mile and cooled off as they went. The air was mild; the noonday sun was warm; both of them had taken off their coats.

They sat down under a clump of basswoods, the only trees beyond the foot of the sand-slope, and looked at the water.

тАЬItтАЩs like a big, useless bathtub,тАЭ observed Randolph.

тАЬNot so much useless as unused.тАЭ

тАЬYes, I suppose the season is as good as overтБатАФthough this end of the lake stays warm longer than most other parts.тАЭ

тАЬIt isnтАЩt so much the warmth of the water,тАЭ remarked Cope sententiously. тАЬItтАЩs more the warmth of the air.тАЭ

тАЬWell, the air seems warm enough. After all, the air and the sun are about the best part of a swim. Do you want to go in?тАЭ

Cope rose, walked to the edge of the water, and put in a finger or two. тАЬWell, it might be warmer; but, as I say.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬWe could try a ten-minute dip. That would get us to our dinner in good time and in good trim.тАЭ

тАЬAll right. LetтАЩs, then.тАЭ

тАЬOnly, youтАЩll have to do most of the swimming,тАЭ said Randolph. тАЬMy few small feats are all accomplished pretty close to shore.тАЭ

тАЬNever mind. CompanyтАЩs the thing. A fellow finds it rather slow, going in alone.тАЭ

Cope whisked off his clothes with incredible rapidity and piled themтБатАФor flung themтБатАФunder the basswoods: the suddenly resuscitated technique of the small-town lad who could take avail of any pond or any quiet stretch of river on the spur of the moment. He waded in quickly up to his waist, and then took an intrepid header. His lithe young legs and arms threw themselves about hither and yon. After a moment or two he got on his feet and made his way back across a yard of fine shingle to the sand itself. He was sputtering and gasping, and the long yellow hair, which usually lay in a flat clean sweep from forehead to occiput, now sprawled in a grotesque pattern round his temples.

тАЬB-r-r! It is cold, sure enough. But jump in. The air will be all right. IтАЩll be back with you in a moment.тАЭ

Randolph advanced to the edge, and felt in turn. It was cold. But he meant to manage it here, just as he had managed with the sand-slopes.

Two heads bobbed on the water where but one had bobbed before. Ceremonially, at least, the rite was complete.

тАЬItтАЩs never so cold the second time,тАЭ declared Cope encouragingly. тАЬOne dip doesnтАЩt make a swim, any more than one swallowтБатАФтАЭ

He flashed his soles in the sunlight and was once again immersed, gulping, in a maelstrom of his own making.

тАЬTwice, to oblige you,тАЭ said Randolph. тАЬBut no more. IтАЩll leave the rest to the sun and the air.тАЭ

Cope, out again, ran up and down the sands for a hundred feet or so. тАЬI know something better than this,тАЭ he declared presently. He threw himself down and rolled himself in the abundance of fine, dry, clean sand.

тАЬAn arenaceous ulsterтБатАФspeaking etymologically,тАЭ he said. He came back to the clump of basswoods near which Randolph was sitting on a short length of drift wood, with his back to the sun, and sat down beside him.

тАЬYouтАЩre welcome to it,тАЭ said Randolph, laughing; тАЬbut how are you going to get it off? By another dip? Certainly not by the slow process of time. We have some moments to spare, but hardly enough for that. Meanwhile.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

He picked up a handful of sand and applied it to a bare shoulder-blade which somehow had failed to get its share of protection.

тАЬThanks,тАЭ said Cope: тАЬthe right thing done for Polynices. Yes, I shall take one final dip and dry myself on my handkerchief.тАЭ

тАЬI shall dry by the other process, and so shall be able to spare you mine.тАЭ

тАЬHow much time have we yet?тАЭ

Randolph reached for his trousers, as they hung on a lower branch of one of the basswoods. тАЬOh, a good three-quarters of an hour.тАЭ

тАЬThatтАЩs time enough, and to spare. I wonder whom weтАЩre going to meet.тАЭ

тАЬThereтАЩs a тАШusual crowdтАЩ: the three young ladies, commonly; one or two young men who understand how to tinker the oil-stoveтБатАФwhich usually needs itтБатАФand how to prime the pump. They once asked me to do these things; but IтАЩve discovered that younger men enjoy it more than I do, so I let them do it. Besides these, a number of miscellaneous people, perhaps, who come out by trolley or in their own cars.тАЭ

тАЬThe young ladies always come?тАЭ asked Cope, brushing the sand from his chest.

тАЬUsually. Together. The Graces. Otherwise, what becomes of the Group?тАЭ

тАЬWell, I hope thereтАЩll be enough fellows to look after the stove and the pumpтБатАФand them. IтАЩm not much good at that last.тАЭ

тАЬNo?тАЭ

тАЬThereтАЩs a knack about itтБатАФa techniqueтБатАФthat I donтАЩt seem to possess. Nor do I seem greatly prompted to learn it.тАЭ

тАЬOf course, there is no more reason for assuming that every man will make a good lover than that every woman will make a good mother or a good housekeeper.тАЭ

тАЬOr that every adult male will make a good citizen, desiring the general welfare and bestirring himself to contribute his own share to it. I donтАЩt feel that IтАЩm an especially creditable one.тАЭ

тАЬSo it runs. We ground our general life on theories, and then the facts come up and slap us in the face.тАЭ Randolph rose and relieved the basswood of the first garments. тАЬAre you about ready for that final dip?тАЭ

Cope made his last plunge and returned red and shivering to use the two handkerchiefs.

тАЬWell, we have thirty minutes,тАЭ said Randolph, as they resumed their march. On the one hand the ragged line of dunes with their draping, dense or slight, of pines, lindens and oaks; on the other the unruffled expanse of blue, spreading toward a horizon even less determinate than before.

тАЬNo, IтАЩm not at all apt,тАЭ said Cope, returning to his theme; тАЬnot even for self-defense. I suppose IтАЩm pretty sure to get caught some time or other.тАЭ

тАЬEach woman according to her powers and gifts. Varying degrees of desire, of determination, of dexterity. To be just, I might add a fourth dтБатАФdevotion.тАЭ

тАЬYouтАЩve run the gauntlet,тАЭ said Cope. тАЬYou seem to have come through all right.тАЭ

тАЬWell,тАЭ Randolph returned deprecatingly, тАЬI canтАЩt really claim ever to have enlisted any womanтАЩs best endeavors.тАЭ

тАЬI hope I shall have the same good luck. Of your four dтАЩs, itтАЩs the dexterity that gives me the most dread.тАЭ

тАЬYes, the appeal (not always honest) to chivalryтБатАФthough devotion is sometimes a close second. YouтАЩre maneuvered into a position where youтАЩre made to think you тАШmust.тАЩ IтАЩve known chaps to marry on that basis.тБатАКтБатАж ItтАЩs weary waiting until Madame dies and Madonna steps into her place.тАЭ

тАЬMeanwhile, safety in numbers.тАЭ

тАЬYes, even though youтАЩre in the very midst of wishing or of wonderingтБатАФor of a careful concern to cloak either.тАЭ

тАЬDonтАЩt dwell on it! You fill me with apprehensions.тАЭ

Randolph put up his arm and pointed. A roof through a notch between two sandhills beyond a long range of them, was seen, set high and half hidden by the spreading limbs of pines. тАЬThere it is,тАЭ he said.

тАЬSo close, already?тАЭ Such, indeed, it appeared.

тАЬNot so close as it seems. We may just as well step lively.тАЭ

Cope, with an abundance of free action, was treading along on the very edge of things, careless of the rough shingle and indifferent to the probability of wet feet, and swinging his hat as he went. In some such spirit, perhaps, advanced young Stoutheart to the ogreтАЩs castle. He even began to foot it a little faster.

тАЬWell, I can keep up with you yet,тАЭ thought Randolph. Aloud, he said: тАЬYouтАЩve done very well with your hair. Quite an inspiration to have carried a comb.тАЭ

Cope grimaced.

тАЬI trust IтАЩm free to comb myself on Sunday. There are plenty of others to do it for me through the week.тАЭ
